text:
INITIAL EVALUATION NOTE — PRIMARY CARE / URGENT REFERRAL

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Internal Medicine / Primary Care
DEPARTMENT: General Internal Medicine
VISIT TYPE: Office Visit — Urgent

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Progressive jaundice, unintentional weight loss, epigastric pain radiating to the back, and pale greasy stools over the past 10 weeks.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old Ghanaian-American woman, retired librarian, who presents today with a 10-week history of what she initially attributed to "stomach trouble" but which has progressively worsened to include painless jaundice, significant unintentional weight loss, and new epigastric discomfort. She reports that family members first noticed yellowing of her eyes approximately 8 weeks ago, though she herself did not seek evaluation at that time, hoping it would resolve on its own. Over the past 3 weeks, the jaundice has become more pronounced and she has noted darkening of her urine (described as "cola-colored") and pale, greasy, malodorous stools that float in the toilet. She endorses diffuse pruritus, particularly bothersome at night, which she initially attributed to dry skin.

She reports a 14-pound unintentional weight loss over the past 2 months (current weight 138 lbs, prior baseline approximately 152 lbs). She has had markedly decreased appetite, describing early satiety and nausea without vomiting. She denies frank hematemesis or melena, though stool color has been pale as described above.

The epigastric discomfort is described as a dull, aching pressure, rated 4-5/10 at baseline, with radiation to the mid-back. Pain is not clearly related to food intake but seems slightly worse after eating fatty meals. She denies any acute severe pain episodes. She has not taken any NSAIDs or new over-the-counter medications.

She denies fever or chills. She has not had prior episodes of pancreatitis, cholelithiasis, or biliary colic. She has never had an ERCP or prior abdominal surgery. She denies alcohol use. She reports mild fatigue that has worsened over the past several weeks, making it difficult to maintain her usual activities.

She denies any recent travel, sick contacts, or known hepatitis exposure. She has had no prior liver disease, hepatitis B or C infection, or autoimmune hepatitis.

Given the constellation of findings — painless obstructive jaundice, significant weight loss, new-onset back pain, and steatorrhea — there is high clinical concern for a pancreatic or biliary malignancy and urgent further workup has been initiated.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Significant fatigue, 14-pound unintentional weight loss over approximately 8 weeks, decreased appetite, early satiety. Denies fever, chills, or night sweats.
Gastrointestinal: Epigastric discomfort radiating to the back as described. Pale, greasy, floating stools. Dark urine. Nausea without vomiting. Denies hematemesis, melena, or hematochezia. Denies change in bowel frequency. No known history of ulcer disease.
Hepatic/Biliary: Scleral icterus and skin jaundice present 8 weeks. Generalized pruritus.
Musculoskeletal: Mild generalized weakness. Denies arthralgias or myalgias.
Neurological: Denies confusion, headache, focal weakness, or numbness.
Cardiovascular: Denies chest pain, palpitations, orthopnea, or lower extremity edema.
Respiratory: Denies cough, shortness of breath, or hemoptysis.
Endocrine: Known type 2 diabetes mellitus, diet-controlled; no recent significant changes in glucose monitoring though she notes she has been eating less. Hypertension, stable on medications.
Dermatologic: Generalized pruritus as noted; no rash.
Genitourinary: Dark urine as described. Denies dysuria or hematuria.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diet-controlled; HbA1c last checked approximately 12 months ago at 6.7%.
2. Hypertension — on amlodipine 5 mg daily; well controlled.
3. Former smoker — 15 pack-year history; quit approximately 10 years ago.
4. No history of pancreatitis, cholelithiasis, or prior abdominal surgery.
5. No prior malignancy.
6. Colonoscopy 5 years ago — no polyps identified.
7. Mammography and Pap smears up to date per primary care records — unremarkable.

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:
No prior abdominal or thoracic surgeries. Cesarean section approximately 30 years ago (remote history).

FAMILY HISTORY:
Mother: deceased, cause unknown (died in Ghana, limited records available).
Father: deceased, hypertension and stroke.
Siblings: two sisters, one brother — no known malignancies reported by the patient.
No known family history of pancreatic cancer, breast cancer, ovarian cancer, or colorectal cancer. No known family history of BRCA-related or Lynch syndrome-related cancers. The patient acknowledges limited knowledge of extended family medical history given distance and communication barriers.

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Marital status: Widowed (husband deceased 5 years ago, cardiac event).
Living situation: Lives alone in a single-story home; two adult children locally who are actively involved.
Occupation: Retired librarian; volunteers part-time at the local public library.
Ethnicity: Ghanaian descent; emigrated to the United States approximately 35 years ago.
Alcohol: Denies alcohol use.
Tobacco: Former smoker, 15 pack-year history, quit 10 years ago.
Illicit substances: Denies.
Diet: Primarily Ghanaian/West African dietary patterns; notes recent marked decrease in oral intake.
Exercise: Previously walked daily; activity significantly curtailed over the past 6-8 weeks due to fatigue.
Functional status: ECOG Performance Status 1 — ambulatory, capable of all self-care, unable to carry out work activities; up and about more than 50% of waking hours.
Health literacy: High; engaged patient who asks thoughtful questions.

ALLERGIES:
No known drug allergies (NKDA).

MEDICATIONS:
1. Amlodipine 5 mg orally once daily (hypertension)
2. Multivitamin 1 tablet orally once daily
3. No anticoagulants, antiplatelet agents, or herbal supplements reported
(Patient is not on metformin or any hypoglycemic agents — diabetes managed with diet alone at this visit)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
General: Alert, well-groomed, moderately ill-appearing woman in no acute distress. Appears thin and slightly fatigued. Speech is clear and cooperative.
Vital Signs: Temperature 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure 136/82 mmHg, heart rate 78 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Weight 138 lbs (62.6 kg), BMI 22.4 kg/m2 (down from reported baseline BMI ~24.5).
Skin: Diffuse icterus of the skin. Mild excoriation marks on bilateral forearms and torso consistent with chronic pruritus. No spider angiomata or palmar erythema. No petechiae or ecchymosis.
Head, Eyes, Ears, Nose, Throat: Scleral icterus bilaterally, moderately prominent. No conjunctival pallor. Oral mucosa moist. No oral lesions or lymphadenopathy in the head and neck.
Lymph Nodes: No cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy palpated. No palpable left supraclavicular (Virchow's) node.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No lower extremity edema.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or crackles.
Abdomen:
  — Abdomen mildly distended in the epigastrium.
  — Soft overall; mild tenderness to deep palpation in the epigastric region and right upper quadrant without peritoneal signs (no guarding or rebound).
  — Hepatomegaly present: liver edge palpable approximately 4 cm below the right costal margin in the midclavicular line; smooth, non-tender edge.
  — A non-tender, smooth, palpable mass is noted in the right upper quadrant just below the liver edge, consistent with a distended gallbladder (Courvoisier sign).
  — No splenomegaly appreciated.
  — No ascites clinically (no fluid wave, shifting dullness absent).
  — Bowel sounds normoactive.
Musculoskeletal: No focal bony tenderness. No joint swelling. Mild generalized muscle wasting noted in bilateral upper and lower extremities.
Neurological: Alert and oriented x4. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all extremities. Sensation intact. No asterixis.
Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  — Sodium: 138 mEq/L
  — Potassium: 4.0 mEq/L
  — Chloride: 101 mEq/L
  — Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
  — BUN: 14 mg/dL
  — Creatinine: 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2)
  — Glucose: 142 mg/dL (fasting)
  — Total bilirubin: 8.2 mg/dL (H)
  — Direct (conjugated) bilirubin: 6.9 mg/dL (H)
  — Indirect bilirubin: 1.3 mg/dL
  — Alkaline phosphatase (ALP): 420 U/L (H)
  — AST: 98 U/L (H)
  — ALT: 112 U/L (H)
  — GGT: 312 U/L (H)
  — Total protein: 6.4 g/dL
  — Albumin: 3.1 g/dL (L)
  — LDH: 198 U/L (normal)
Complete Blood Count:
  — WBC: 7,200/mcL (normal differential)
  — Hemoglobin: 11.4 g/dL (L) — mild normocytic anemia
  — Hematocrit: 34%
  — MCV: 88 fL
  — Platelets: 342,000/mcL
Coagulation:
  — PT/INR: 1.2 (mildly elevated, consistent with mild hepatic synthetic dysfunction or nutritional vitamin K deficiency secondary to biliary obstruction)
Tumor Markers:
  — CA 19-9: 1,204 U/mL (markedly elevated; reference range <37 U/mL)
  — CEA: 7.1 ng/mL (mildly elevated; reference range <3.0 ng/mL)
Urinalysis: Bilirubin 3+ (conjugated bilirubinuria), urobilinogen absent, consistent with complete or near-complete biliary obstruction.

---

IMAGING RESULTS (reviewed at this visit):
No imaging has been performed at this time. A pancreatic protocol CT abdomen and pelvis with triple-phase intravenous contrast has been ordered urgently and is being arranged for completion within the next 24-48 hours. An abdominal ultrasound was considered as a screening study but given the high pre-test probability for pancreatic pathology, a dedicated pancreatic protocol CT is being ordered directly as the more diagnostically informative initial cross-sectional imaging study.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

ASSESSMENT:
Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old woman with diet-controlled type 2 diabetes and hypertension who presents with a highly concerning constellation of findings: 10-week progressive painless obstructive jaundice with direct hyperbilirubinemia, significant unintentional weight loss of 14 pounds, epigastric pain radiating to the back, steatorrhea, and markedly elevated CA 19-9 at 1,204 U/mL. The presence of a palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier sign) in the setting of painless jaundice is a classic finding pointing toward malignant biliary obstruction at or distal to the common bile duct — the pancreatic head is the most likely primary site. The degree of CA 19-9 elevation (>30x the upper limit of normal) combined with the clinical picture is highly concerning for pancreatic malignancy, most likely pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. Choledocholithiasis alone is an unlikely explanation given the absence of prior biliary symptoms, the degree of weight loss, the degree of CA 19-9 elevation, and the presence of the Courvoisier sign.

Differential diagnosis includes:
1. Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (primary concern, most likely)
2. Distal cholangiocarcinoma (biliary tree primary)
3. Ampullary carcinoma
4. Duodenal carcinoma
5. Autoimmune pancreatitis / IgG4-related disease (less likely given marked weight loss and tumor marker elevation, but cannot be excluded without imaging and IgG4 level)
6. Benign biliary stricture (unlikely given clinical picture)

PLAN:

1. Urgent pancreatic protocol CT abdomen and pelvis with triple-phase IV contrast: Ordered today; to be completed within 24-48 hours. Patient instructed on importance of NOT missing this study.

2. Additional bloodwork ordered: IgG4 level (to evaluate for autoimmune pancreatitis as alternative diagnosis), hepatitis B surface antigen and hepatitis B core antibody (prior to any planned treatments), hepatitis C antibody, CA 125, amylase, lipase.

3. Urgent referral to Gastroenterology: Placed today for endoscopic evaluation (ERCP and/or EUS), both for diagnostic purposes (tissue sampling) and potential therapeutic biliary decompression. Given bilirubin of 8.2 mg/dL and a CA 19-9 of 1,204 U/mL, rapid evaluation is paramount. The gastroenterology colleague has been contacted directly by telephone and has agreed to see the patient within 48-72 hours.

4. Urgent referral to Hepatobiliary Surgery / Surgical Oncology: Placed concurrently given the possibility of surgically resectable or borderline resectable disease and the importance of early surgical evaluation. The pancreatic surgery team has been notified.

5. Referral to Medical Oncology: Placed for early involvement and coordination of multidisciplinary care.

6. Nutritional support: Patient counseled on high-calorie, high-protein diet. Referral to clinical dietitian placed. Given the degree of malnutrition and weight loss, early nutritional optimization is critical, particularly if surgery becomes a consideration.

7. Symptom management:
   — Pruritus: Cholestyramine 4 g orally twice daily prescribed for symptomatic relief of cholestatic pruritus while awaiting biliary decompression; patient counseled that this is temporary symptomatic management.
   — Pain: Acetaminophen 500 mg as needed for mild pain; patient counseled to avoid NSAIDs given elevated bilirubin and potential coagulopathy.

8. Glycemic monitoring: Patient counseled to monitor blood glucose more closely given illness-related hyperglycemia (fasting glucose 142 mg/dL today). Will reassess need for temporary pharmacologic management at follow-up.

9. Patient education and emotional support: Extensive time spent with the patient explaining the concern for possible pancreatic malignancy. She was informed of the diagnostic steps being taken and the importance of prompt evaluation. She was accompanied by her daughter today, who was present for the discussion. Patient and family were thoughtful and appropriately distressed but expressed their desire to understand the situation fully and proceed with workup expeditiously. Appropriate anticipatory guidance was provided. Social work referral offered; patient deferred but was given contact information.

10. Follow-up: Patient to return or be contacted within 48-72 hours with imaging results. Urgent communication pathway established with GI and surgical colleagues. If imaging demonstrates findings consistent with pancreatic malignancy with biliary obstruction, prompt ERCP/EUS with tissue sampling and biliary stenting will be arranged.

---

Time spent with patient: approximately 55 minutes (>50% counseling and coordination of care).

Documented by: Attending, General Internal Medicine
